facts: The Sun contains about 99.86% of the total mass of the entire Solar System.
The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star, often classified as a yellow dwarf.
Energy is produced in the Sun's core through nuclear fusion, converting hydrogen into helium.
The Sun's core reaches temperatures of roughly 15 million degrees Celsius.
The Sun's visible surface, the photosphere, is about 5,500 degrees Celsius.
About 4 million tonnes of mass are converted into energy in the Sun every second.
Light from the Sun takes roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth.
The Sun is composed mostly of hydrogen (about 73%) and helium (about 25%) by mass.
The Sun is around 4.6 billion years old and is roughly halfway through its main-sequence life.
More than one million Earths could fit inside the volume of the Sun.
The Sun's outer atmosphere, the corona, is mysteriously far hotter than its surface, reaching over a million degrees.
The Sun rotates faster at its equator (about 25 days) than near its poles (about 35 days).
Sunspots are cooler, darker regions on the photosphere caused by intense magnetic activity.
The Sun's magnetic field flips its polarity roughly every 11 years in the solar cycle.
The constant outflow of charged particles from the Sun is called the solar wind.
Solar flares and coronal mass ejections can disrupt satellites and power grids on Earth.
In about 5 billion years the Sun will expand into a red giant, likely engulfing the inner planets.
After its red giant phase, the Sun will shed its outer layers and become a white dwarf.
The Sun's gravity holds the entire Solar System together, including planets, comets, and asteroids.
The interaction of solar wind with Earth's magnetic field produces the auroras near the poles.
Mercury is the smallest planet in the Solar System, only slightly larger than Earth's Moon.
Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of about 58 million kilometers.
Mercury completes one orbit around the Sun in just 88 Earth days.
Mercury has a 3:2 spin-orbit resonance, rotating exactly three times for every two orbits it makes.
A single day-night cycle on Mercury lasts about 176 Earth days, longer than its own year.
Despite being closest to the Sun, Mercury is not the hottest planet; Venus is hotter.
Temperatures on Mercury swing from about 430 degrees Celsius in daylight to minus 180 at night.
Mercury has almost no atmosphere, only a thin exosphere of atoms blasted off its surface.
Mercury has a surprisingly large iron core making up a big fraction of its volume.
Mercury is one of only two planets with a global magnetic field among the rocky worlds.
Radar has detected water ice in permanently shadowed craters near Mercury's poles.
Mercury's surface is heavily cratered, resembling the Moon, due to its lack of weathering.
The Caloris Basin is a giant impact crater on Mercury about 1,500 kilometers across.
Mercury has long cliffs called scarps formed as the planet cooled and shrank.
Mercury has no moons and no ring system.
Mercury is named after the swift Roman messenger god, fitting its rapid orbit.
Only two spacecraft have visited Mercury: Mariner 10 and MESSENGER.
The MESSENGER probe orbited Mercury from 2011 to 2015 and mapped its entire surface.
The BepiColombo mission, a joint European-Japanese effort, is en route to study Mercury.
Mercury's orbit is the most eccentric (elliptical) of all the planets.
Venus is the hottest planet in the Solar System, with surface temperatures around 465 degrees Celsius.
A runaway greenhouse effect from its thick carbon dioxide atmosphere traps heat on Venus.
Venus's atmosphere is about 96% carbon dioxide with clouds of sulfuric acid.
The atmospheric pressure on Venus's surface is about 90 times that of Earth, like being deep underwater.
Venus rotates backwards (retrograde) compared to most planets, so the Sun rises in the west.
A single day on Venus (one rotation) is longer than its entire year.
Venus takes about 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 days to orbit the Sun.
Venus is often called Earth's twin because of its similar size, mass, and composition.
Venus is the brightest natural object in our night sky after the Moon.
Venus is sometimes called the morning star or evening star depending on its position.
Venus has no moons and no rings.
The surface of Venus is hidden beneath permanent thick clouds, requiring radar to map it.
Venus has thousands of volcanoes, more than any other planet in the Solar System.
Maxwell Montes on Venus is its highest mountain range, taller than Mount Everest.
Venus is named after the Roman goddess of love and beauty.
The Soviet Venera probes were the first to land on Venus and send back surface images.
Landers on Venus survive only a couple of hours before being destroyed by heat and pressure.
Venus lacks a significant magnetic field despite its similar size to Earth.
High-altitude winds on Venus whip around the planet far faster than the planet itself rotates.
Venus's surface is relatively young, likely resurfaced by widespread volcanic activity.
Earth is the only known planet to support life.
About 71% of Earth's surface is covered by liquid water.
Earth is the only planet known to have liquid water stable on its surface.
Earth's atmosphere is about 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen.
Earth's protective ozone layer absorbs most of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation.
Earth has a strong global magnetic field that shields it from harmful solar radiation.
Earth's magnetic field is generated by motion in its liquid iron outer core.
Earth completes one rotation in about 23 hours and 56 minutes relative to the stars.
Earth orbits the Sun once every 365.25 days, which is why we add a leap day every four years.
Earth is the densest planet in the Solar System.
Earth has a single large natural satellite, the Moon.
The Moon's gravity is the main cause of Earth's ocean tides.
Earth's axis is tilted about 23.5 degrees, which gives us the seasons.
Mount Everest is Earth's highest peak above sea level at about 8,849 meters.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest point in Earth's oceans, nearly 11 kilometers down.
Earth is the only planet not named after a Greek or Roman deity.
Plate tectonics constantly reshape Earth's surface, creating mountains, earthquakes, and volcanoes.
Earth is about 4.5 billion years old, formed from the same cloud of material as the Sun.
Earth is the largest of the four rocky inner planets.
Seen from space, Earth appears as a blue marble due to its oceans and atmosphere.
Mars is known as the Red Planet because iron oxide (rust) gives its surface a reddish color.
Mars is home to Olympus Mons, the tallest known volcano in the Solar System at about 22 kilometers high.
Valles Marineris on Mars is a canyon system over 4,000 kilometers long, dwarfing the Grand Canyon.
Mars has two small moons named Phobos and Deimos.
Phobos orbits so close to Mars that it is slowly spiraling inward and will eventually break apart.
A day on Mars, called a sol, is about 24 hours and 39 minutes, close to Earth's day.
Mars takes about 687 Earth days to complete one orbit around the Sun.
Mars has a thin atmosphere made mostly of carbon dioxide.
Mars experiences planet-wide dust storms that can last for weeks or months.
Mars has polar ice caps made of water ice and frozen carbon dioxide (dry ice).
Evidence shows liquid water once flowed across the surface of ancient Mars.
Mars is about half the diameter of Earth.
Surface temperatures on Mars average around minus 60 degrees Celsius.
Mars is named after the Roman god of war because of its blood-red color.
Mars lacks a global magnetic field, leaving its surface exposed to solar radiation.
Several rovers, including Curiosity and Perseverance, are actively exploring Mars.
NASA's Ingenuity helicopter achieved the first powered flight on another planet on Mars.
Mars has the largest dust devils and some of the most dramatic terrain in the Solar System.
Mars's gravity is only about 38% of Earth's, so you would weigh much less there.
Mars's reddish appearance has made it visible and notable to skywatchers since ancient times.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System, more massive than all other planets combined.
Jupiter is a gas giant composed mostly of hydrogen and helium, similar to the Sun.
The Great Red Spot on Jupiter is a giant storm that has raged for at least 350 years.
The Great Red Spot is so large that Earth could fit inside it.
Jupiter has the shortest day of any planet, rotating once in under 10 hours.
Jupiter's rapid rotation gives it a noticeably flattened, oblate shape.
Jupiter has the strongest magnetic field of any planet, far more powerful than Earth's.
Jupiter has at least 95 confirmed moons, the most of any planet.
Jupiter's four largest moons, Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto, are called the Galilean moons.
Ganymede is the largest moon in the Solar System, bigger than the planet Mercury.
Io is the most volcanically active body in the Solar System.
Europa may hide a salty ocean beneath its icy crust, a possible home for life.
Jupiter has a faint ring system made mostly of dust.
Jupiter takes about 12 Earth years to orbit the Sun once.
Jupiter is named after the king of the Roman gods.
Galileo Galilei discovered Jupiter's four large moons in 1610.
Jupiter's powerful gravity helps shield the inner planets by deflecting many comets and asteroids.
NASA's Juno spacecraft is currently studying Jupiter's atmosphere and interior.
Jupiter emits more heat than it receives from the Sun, due to slow gravitational contraction.
Jupiter's banded appearance comes from fast-moving cloud belts of different chemical compositions.
Saturn is famous for its spectacular and extensive ring system.
Saturn's rings are made mostly of countless particles of water ice plus some rock.
Saturn is the least dense planet in the Solar System and would float in water if there were a big enough ocean.
Saturn is the second largest planet, a gas giant after Jupiter.
Saturn has at least 140 confirmed moons, among the most of any planet.
Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is bigger than the planet Mercury and has a thick atmosphere.
Titan has lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane on its surface.
Enceladus, a moon of Saturn, shoots geysers of water vapor from a subsurface ocean.
Saturn's rings span up to about 280,000 kilometers but are only tens of meters thick in places.
A day on Saturn lasts only about 10.7 hours due to its fast rotation.
Saturn takes about 29 Earth years to complete one orbit around the Sun.
Saturn has a hexagon-shaped storm pattern at its north pole.
Saturn is composed mainly of hydrogen and helium like Jupiter.
Saturn is named after the Roman god of agriculture and wealth.
Saturn's rings will appear to vanish from Earth's view periodically when seen edge-on.
The Cassini spacecraft orbited Saturn for 13 years, ending with a dramatic plunge into the planet.
Saturn's winds can reach speeds of around 1,800 kilometers per hour.
Galileo first observed Saturn's rings in 1610 but could not tell what they were.
Saturn's rings may be relatively young, possibly only a few hundred million years old.
Saturn is the most distant planet easily visible to the naked eye from Earth.
Uranus is tipped on its side, with an axial tilt of about 98 degrees.
Because of its extreme tilt, Uranus essentially orbits the Sun lying on its side.
Each pole of Uranus gets about 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness.
Uranus is an ice giant, made largely of water, methane, and ammonia ices.
Methane in Uranus's atmosphere gives the planet its pale blue-green color.
Uranus is the coldest planet in the Solar System, with temperatures dropping below minus 220 degrees Celsius.
Uranus was the first planet discovered with a telescope, by William Herschel in 1781.
Uranus takes about 84 Earth years to orbit the Sun once.
A day on Uranus lasts about 17 hours, and it rotates in a retrograde direction.
Uranus has a faint set of dark, narrow rings.
Uranus has 27 known moons, named after characters from Shakespeare and Alexander Pope.
Miranda, a moon of Uranus, has some of the most bizarre and varied terrain in the Solar System.
Uranus is named after the ancient Greek god of the sky, unlike most Roman-named planets.
Only one spacecraft, Voyager 2, has ever flown past Uranus, in 1986.
Uranus is about four times the diameter of Earth.
Uranus's magnetic field is strangely tilted and offset from the planet's center.
The extreme tilt of Uranus may have been caused by a massive ancient collision.
Uranus appears nearly featureless in visible light compared to the banded gas giants.
Winds on Uranus can blow at hundreds of kilometers per hour.
Uranus is barely visible to the naked eye under very dark skies.
Neptune is the most distant planet from the Sun in the Solar System.
Neptune has the fastest winds in the Solar System, reaching supersonic speeds over 2,000 kilometers per hour.
Neptune is an ice giant composed of water, methane, and ammonia around a rocky core.
Methane in Neptune's atmosphere gives it a deep, vivid blue color.
Neptune was the first planet discovered through mathematical prediction rather than observation.
Neptune was found in 1846, very close to the position predicted by calculations.
Neptune takes about 165 Earth years to complete a single orbit around the Sun.
Neptune completed its first full orbit since discovery only in 2011.
A day on Neptune lasts about 16 hours.
Neptune once had a storm called the Great Dark Spot, similar to Jupiter's Great Red Spot.
Neptune has at least 14 known moons.
Triton, Neptune's largest moon, orbits backwards and may be a captured dwarf planet.
Triton is one of the coldest known objects in the Solar System, with active nitrogen geysers.
Neptune has a faint, clumpy ring system.
Neptune is named after the Roman god of the sea, fitting its blue color.
Only one spacecraft, Voyager 2, has ever visited Neptune, flying by in 1989.
Neptune radiates more than twice as much heat as it receives from the distant Sun.
Neptune is about four times the diameter of Earth and 17 times more massive.
Neptune is too faint to be seen with the naked eye and requires a telescope.
Despite being farther out, Neptune is slightly smaller but denser than Uranus.
